perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Receiving Party will, within two (2) weeks of written requestfrom Disclosing Party, return all documents concerning the Proprietary Information and NRE L P rotected Information and all copies of any such documents to Disclosing Party, or certify i n w riting their destruction, with the exception of copies of Proprietary Information and NRE L P rotected Information made as a matter of routine information technology or legal backup, provided that such copies will continue to be subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth i n t his Agreement and may only be used in resolving a dispute between the Parties regarding this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Receiving Party will, within sixty (60) days of written request from Disclosing Party, return all documents concerning the Proprietary Information and NRE L P rotected Information and all copies of any such documents to Disclosing Party, or certify i n w riting their destruction, with the exception of copies of Proprietary Information and NRE L P rotected Information made as a matter of routine information technology or legal backup, provided that such copies will continue to be subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth i n t his Agreement and may only be used in resolving a dispute between the Parties regarding this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period to 60 days may contradict state laws that require the prompt return of confidential information upon termination of an agreement. Although this NDA is executed in Colorado, many states have laws regarding trade secrets and prompt return of proprietary information. For example, some states may consider 60 days an unreasonable delay that violates the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA does not explicitly define a timeframe, delaying the return of trade secret information by setting an unreasonable timeframe like 60 days, could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially violating CUTSA. The 'reasonable measures' provision implies that the return should be prompt upon termination.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'two (2) weeks' to 'sixty (60) days' for returning proprietary information creates a potential legal contradiction. While the provided law snippets are insufficient to definitively determine the legality, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight concerns that a 60-day delay might be considered unreasonable and a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially violating CUTSA. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation in the contract does not align with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause by giving the other Part y thirty ( 30)da ys prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause by giving the other Party ten (10) days prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 10 days could conflict with laws requiring reasonable notice for contract termination, depending on the specific industry and nature of the agreement. Although this NDA is executed in Colorado, depending on the type of agreement the NDA relates to, Colorado's common law may require more than 10 days notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Colorado Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: Colorado law on contract termination and the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. A ten-day notice period for termination might be deemed unreasonable or in bad faith, especially if significant investments or dependencies exist based on the agreement. The court will look at the type of contract, the parties' understanding, and the length of the relationship to determine if 10 days is commercially fair.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that Colorado law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, and a ten-day notice period might be deemed unreasonable, especially if significant investments exist. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that the reduced notice period may conflict with laws requiring reasonable notice, depending on the agreement's nature. While neither snippet contains explicit law, the arguments in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that reducing the notice period to 10 days could be deemed unreasonable and therefore a contradiction based on implied covenant of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: The “Confidentiality Period” for this Agreement is three (3) years from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The “Confidentiality Period” for this Agreement is one (1) year from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could potentially conflict with trade secret law, particularly if the information disclosed requires a longer period of protection to maintain its competitive advantage. If the information remains a trade secret beyond one year, a one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to protect it under the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, trade secrets must be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain their secrecy. If the nature of the proprietary information is such that it would likely retain its trade secret status beyond a year, setting a confidentiality period of only one year might be viewed as failing to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, particularly if that trade secret retains its economic value derived from not being generally known or readily ascertainable for longer than that time.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could conflict with trade secret law if the information requires longer protection. Even though there are no scraped law snippets, the explanations provided detail that under CUTSA, reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy, and a one-year confidentiality period might not be sufficient if the trade secret's value persists longer than that. The change directly contradicts the implicit obligation to adequately protect trade secrets for a duration that maintains their value.

perturbation.original_text: For Proprietary Information or NRELProtected Information first disclosed orally (i.e., information expressed by spoken words) hereunder, Disclosing Party will : (i) identify such information as Proprietary Information or NRE L P rotected Information, as applicable, at the time it is conveyed to Receiving Party; (ii) reduc e EXAMPLE Bilateral (IN and OUT) NDA NREL No. 18- XXXXX Page 2 of 3 such information to writing; and (iii) provide an appropriately identified and marked copy of such writing to Receiving Party within thirty (30) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: For Proprietary Information or NRELProtected Information first disclosed orally (i.e., information expressed by spoken words) hereunder, Disclosing Party will : (i) identify such information as Proprietary Information or NRE L P rotected Information, as applicable, at the time it is conveyed to Receiving Party; (ii) reduce EXAMPLE Bilateral (IN and OUT) NDA NREL No. 18- XXXXX Page 2 of 3 such information to writing; and (iii) provide an appropriately identified and marked copy of such writing to Receiving Party within ninety (90) days of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period for reducing orally disclosed information to writing to 90 days may be deemed unreasonable and could jeopardize trade secret protection under CUTSA. A significant delay in documenting orally disclosed information may be viewed as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, protecting a trade secret involves taking reasonable measures to maintain its secrecy. Waiting 90 days to document oral disclosures could be deemed unreasonable, especially if the information is sensitive and easily misappropriated. This delay increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or loss of confidentiality, potentially undermining trade secret protection. Promptly documenting oral disclosures is typically considered a best practice for maintaining trade secret status, and a 90-day delay could be seen as a departure from this standard.
perturbation.justification: The change extends the time frame for documenting oral disclosures from 30 to 90 days. According to the Perturbation and Law explanations, this delay may be deemed unreasonable and could jeopardize trade secret protection under CUTSA because it increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or loss of confidentiality, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The original text ensures quicker documentation, which aligns better with maintaining secrecy, whereas the changed text introduces a significant delay that contradicts the standard of promptly documenting oral disclosures to maintain trade secret status.
